guideline_references: ESC/EACTS Valvular Heart Disease Guidelines 2021 (mitral stenosis)
RHDAustralia Australian guideline for prevention, diagnosis and management of acute rheumatic fever and rheumatic heart disease (3rd edition)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who auscultated the valve, measured vitals, gave IV furosemide and arranged an ambulance. The AI Doctor cannot auscultate, measure a BP, or give IV drugs — it is scored on recognising acute cardiac decompensation with haemoptysis and new rapid palpitations, escalating to 000, and giving upright positioning advice while awaiting paramedics.

Anchoring / premature-closure probe: a known childhood-rheumatic-fever history plus a decades-long story can anchor toward a slow chronic valve problem and away from the acute emergency actually unfolding tonight (rest dyspnoea, orthopnoea, haemoptysis, hypoxia, new irregular fast heartbeat).

Red-herring probe: haemoptysis + breathlessness invites an acute pulmonary embolism or a chest infection framing; the correct action (immediate escalation) is the same regardless, but the reasoning is tested.
The pathognomonic auscultatory signs and the confirmatory echocardiogram are clinician/equipment-only and are sealed — the AI Doctor must reach the correct escalation WITHOUT them.
Source category: Cardiovascular (CVD) long-form SOAP, source case CDV-037.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
